← the Atlas

Review orchestration — kill-rounds beat debates

analysis · seedling ·proposed ·JUN 11, 2026

An 18-agent research pass over the multi-agent-review literature, kolu's debate skills, and agency's /do reviewer concludes that independent generation plus one cold cross-model kill-round beats debate-to-consensus — with a concrete /do redesign and recommended changes to /be-review, /codex-debate, and /lens-debate.

Contents

  • TL;DR
  • What the evidence says
    • Diversity, not debate, is where the measured payoff lives
    • More rounds make consensus worse, not better
    • For review, the best-evidenced shape is a cold kill-round, not a conversation
    • Redaction is the cheapest large lever
    • Structural-lens diversity is a bet, not a result
  • The design tournament
    • Why debate-centric lost (3–3.5/10)
    • Why workflow-maximalist lost (4.5/10)
    • Why ledger + kill-round won (7/10, both judges)
  • The /do proposal
    • The findings ledger is the spine
    • The codex kill-round
    • The evidence gate computes dispositions; nobody negotiates
    • One bounded post-apply re-review
    • Cross-validation: keep, transformed, in two stages
    • Shipping order
  • What kolu should change
    • /be-review: reorder the gauntlet, fix the rationale channel, add a cheap tier
    • /codex-debate: cap the review loop, gate concessions on evidence
    • /lens-debate: verify unanimity, tighten the backstop

How should hickey/lowy structural review be orchestrated, now that we have Dynamic Workflows for determinism and codex for a second model family? This note is the answer to that question, researched for agency’s /do (which kolu consumes via srid/agency#master in apm.yml:24) and read back against kolu’s own review stack — /be-review, /codex-debate ( #1269 answer mode included), and /lens-debate. 18-agent workflow + adversarial critique

Method: a Claude Code dynamic workflow ran 4 parallel evidence gatherers (kolu’s debate internals, agency’s documented failure history, Workflow-tool capabilities, the academic/practitioner literature), then 3 independent designers from different angles, 9 adversarial critics (cost, quality, portability — three per design), and 2 judges with opposing priorities (merged-code quality vs. pragmatic adoption). Roughly 1.1M subagent tokens. Both judges independently ranked the same design first.

TL;DR

Verdict

Don’t debate to consensus; verify with a cold kill-round. The measured payoff of multi-model review comes from model diversity, not from the debating — and rounds past the first measurably degrade outcomes (sycophantic convergence, bias amplification, unanimous-wrong consensus). The best-evidenced shape for review is: independent parallel generation → merge with stable finding ids → ONE cold cross-model verification round on claims-only packets → an evidence gate that computes dispositions → serial apply → one bounded re-review. Agreement between models is not evidence; only code citations and execution are. For kolu specifically: /be-review should run lens before codex (so the cross-model debate reviews the structural rewrites), thread --rationale into adjudication, not generation, and both debate skills should cap rounds and gate concessions on cited evidence — #1222 is the in-house preview of what the literature predicts.

The proposed pipeline (for agency’s /do; the kolu recommendations in the last section are derived from the same evidence):

QuestionAnswerStrongest evidence
Does Claude ⇄ codex debate improve review?The second model family improves it; the debating mostly doesn’tReConcile attributes gains to diversity, not protocol; independent aggregation beat debate in a Du-et-al follow-up
How many rounds are worth it?One cold cross-check round; gains saturate at two; three is harmfulUnanimous-wrong consensus grows monotonically with rounds (~24% of disputed items by round 3)
What kills false positives?Cold, claims-only adversarial verification with mandatory code citationsRefute-or-Promote killed 79–83% of candidate findings as false positives pre-disclosure
Is hickey ⇄ lowy cross-validation worth keeping?Yes — it has the only empirically validated catch in the stack; transform it behind evidence, don’t delete itagency#198 caught a real cross-lens bug; the codex replacement is an unproven bet for structural claims
Biggest cheap lever?Redaction: never show reviewers the author’s self-assessment or the proposer’s reasoningAuthor framing cut findings 3–4× in a controlled bug-planting study

What the evidence says

Four research threads, fact-checked against primary sources where the claims are load-bearing. Secondhand numbers (search-summary provenance) are flagged; directions are corroborated across multiple sources even where exact figures are not.

Diversity, not debate, is where the measured payoff lives

The foundational multi-agent-debate result (Du et al. 2023) shows 3 agents × 2 rounds with majority vote beating single-model chain-of-thought on math, factuality, and MMLU. But the controlled replication literature is sobering:

  • Smit et al., “Should we be going MAD?” found multi-agent debate does not reliably outperform self-consistency or ensembling at comparable cost without careful tuning. The most promising knob was agreement modulation — controlling how easily agents concede.
  • A controlled study of MAD in logical reasoning found debate adds value only when agents initially disagree; when initial answers are similar, extra rounds add cost, not signal.
  • A follow-up to Du et al. (Diversity of Thought) reports that simply aggregating three models’ independent answers without debate (69%) beat letting them debate (66%) — secondhand numbers, directionally consistent with everything else.
  • ReConcile — a round-table of heterogeneous models with confidence-weighted voting, up to +11.4% over baselines — explicitly attributes its gains to model diversity, not the discussion protocol. This is the strongest academic support for the Claude+codex premise: the payoff comes from the second model family, and most of it is collectable without any debate at all.

More rounds make consensus worse, not better

This is the part that cuts directly against “no round cap, no deadlock exit”:

  • A 2026 study of multi-agent deliberation (arXiv 2604.07667) reports that by round 3, ~24% of initially-disputed questions end in unanimous wrong consensus, increasing monotonically with rounds. “Not All Flips Are Conformity” decomposes stance changes: strict conformity (~29% of flips) is predominantly harmful (57–77% correct→wrong), and agents adopt errors even from vacuous reasoning 20–39% of the time. (Both secondhand; the direction is corroborated.)
  • Judging with Many Minds (EMNLP 2025 Findings) found multi-agent debate amplifies judge biases sharply after the first round (position, verbosity, bandwagon) and the amplification persists; meta-judge setups resist bias better than debate. Survey literature agrees gains saturate at round 2, marginal at round 3.
  • LLM-as-judge baselines are themselves shaky: position bias flips pairwise code judgments on order swap more than 10% of the time (Judging the Judges); self-preference bias correlates with self-recognition (NeurIPS 2024) — a direct argument for cross-family review over same-family second passes.
  • Structural judgments have no oracle — and the domains without checkable answers are precisely where deliberation converged wrong most often. The lens debates operate exactly there.

Worth stating carefully: /lens-debate’s “why deadlock is not possible” argument (.apm/skills/lens-debate/SKILL.md) is about termination — disinterested lenses with no authorship stake do converge. The literature’s complaint is about correctness: they converge, and a measured fraction of the time they converge wrong, at a rate that grows with rounds. Termination and truth are different properties; the round cap defends the second, not the first.

For review, the best-evidenced shape is a cold kill-round, not a conversation

The single most relevant paper to this design space is Refute-or-Promote, a stage-gated adversarial pipeline for defect discovery:

  • Creative agents argue a finding is real; cold-context adversarial agents receive only the claim — not the proposer’s reasoning — and try to kill it; nothing ships without an empirical check.
  • Results: 79–83% of candidate findings were false positives killed before disclosure. The cross-model critic stage caught bugs same-family review missed and found correctness issues in ~16% of same-family-approved fixes.
  • Its hardest-won lesson: “unanimity is a low-signal event.” 80+ agents unanimously endorsed a nonexistent OpenSSL vulnerability (killed by one test), and a unanimous kill of a real bug later became a CVE. Agreement between LLMs is not evidence; only execution is.

Supporting results: Cross-Context Review found fresh-session review beats same-session (anchoring suppresses criticism) and cross-model beats same-model even at equal capability. MAD applied directly to code tasks shows mixed results, and heterogeneous agent teams often underperform their best member due to consensus-seeking over expertise.

Redaction is the cheapest large lever

A controlled bug-planting experiment (Claude reviews codex’s work, 96 reviews) found framing is the biggest quality lever: passing the implementer’s confident self-assessment to the reviewer cut findings 3–4× (9.4 → 2.4–4.0 per review) and halved critical-severity tags. Redaction discipline mattered more than which vendor reviewed.

kolu independently discovered the same effect: /lens-debate’s own #1109 trial found that feeding hickey a pre-curated lowy finding to rebut produced a drop verdict, while independent parallel review made hickey raise the same issue on its own and flip the verdict to fix — “curation biases the outcome; independent-then-debate does not” (.apm/skills/lens-debate/SKILL.md). The literature and the in-house experiment agree. The design consequence: redact the author’s self-assessment and confidence language from generation prompts, and redact the proposing reviewer’s chain of reasoning from verification packets — but always include the full claim text and its cited code locations, or responsible refutation becomes impossible.

Structural-lens diversity is a bet, not a result

Cross-model diversity for bug-finding is real: CodeFuse-CR-Bench finds no single LLM dominates across code-review dimensions; vendor benchmarks claim multi-model review catches ~1/3 more issues with low overlap. But no study directly measures whether frontier models produce different structural findings (decomposition, coupling, volatility) — and a strong lens prompt like hickey/lowy’s may homogenize outputs across model families, shrinking the diversity you’re paying for while inflating false confidence when both families “agree”. The honest position: codex running the hickey lens is an unproven bet, codex verifying hickey-lens claims is the evidenced role. This is testable cheaply — run the kill-round retrospectively over ~10 past merged PRs and count verified refutes/amends/duplicates before building anything permanent on the generation side.

The design tournament

Three designers worked from the same four evidence briefs under different angles; nine adversarial critics attacked each design through one lens each (cost/latency/maintenance, review quality, portability/failure-modes); two judges scored the dossier with opposing priority orderings. Both judges picked the same winner.

Why debate-centric lost (3–3.5/10)

The most architecturally elegant reading of the debate literature — debate only on disagreement, claims-only challenges, progress-gating — but its central mechanism failed both judges’ first criterion:

  • Its corroboration gate auto-applied precisely the findings most likely to be correlated false positives (same diff, same lens catalog, two model families sharing the prompt) with less scrutiny than today, while “unanimity is a low-signal event” says agreement must never reduce scrutiny.
  • Its contested→No-op disposition reinstalled Defer through the back door, with the incentive gradient pointing exactly opposite to the hard-won flip-to-Fix rule (three generations of prompt patching in agency: #55 → #159 → #160 ): refute-then-stall costs two cheap calls; confirming obligates an apply+commit+verify chain.
  • Its cost and quality premises were mutually exclusive: high cross-family finding overlap makes codex generation pure waste; low overlap routes most findings into per-finding debates with serialized warm sessions — re-deriving the #1222 runaway with a termination proof attached.

Why workflow-maximalist lost (4.5/10)

The most complete mechanization toolkit (schema’d no-Defer, role separation in code, the only design confronting the re-review hole) — but as specced:

  • ~19–22 agents and 3–5 codex execs per run, on the step that agency’s timing data ( #128 ) already shows is the slowest in 8/20 runs;
  • a round-1 gate that hard-failed the whole step on a single codex flake, directly contradicting its own soft-degradation story;
  • a stability loop that licenses lens ping-pong and manufactures convergence via gag-order prompts;
  • a ~10-artifact maintenance surface across three languages with the findings schema duplicated — the exact mirror-edit failure mode it cited from kolu’s own codex-debate history, shipped twice.

Why ledger + kill-round won (7/10, both judges)

The winning design structures the data instead of rebuilding the orchestration: a findings ledger every tier writes, one cold codex kill-round replacing the weakest phase, and a thin straight-line workflow wrapping exactly that sequence for Claude Code users.

  • The codex bet is bounded: codex acts only on refute/amend/duplicate verdicts; confirms change nothing versus today; codex absence degrades to today’s baseline verbatim. Worst case approximates the status quo.
  • Cost explosion is structurally impossible: no loops anywhere; fixed agent count (5–6 vs. the maximalist’s ~20); the zero-findings path is cheaper than today.
  • Three independently-landable increments — a solo maintainer can ship the proven part before betting on the rest.
  • Its critiques found genuine criticals — but every one converged on a point-fix (write-ahead ledger, symmetric evidence gate, resequencing) rather than an indictment of the architecture.

The /do proposal

What lands in agency (and therefore in kolu’s installed /do via APM), in recommended shipping order. Full pipeline as in the diagram above; the parts worth spelling out:

The findings ledger is the spine

One gitignored .do/findings.json, written as a write-ahead log: stamped with merge-base sha + run id, persisted at every phase boundary and after each applied commit, with machine-readable provenance trailers (finding id, lens, disposition) on every refactor(hickey|lowy) commit. Every consumer — the PR-table renderer, the fallback path, --from polish re-entry — validates the stamp and refuses stale state. This converts a crashed or interrupted run from a wedge into a resume-by-content, and it makes the no-Defer rule structural: the disposition field is a schema enum of fix-in-this-pr | no-op — the rule that took three generations of prose patching moves into a type that validation retries enforce.

The codex kill-round

ONE cold codex exec per run (not per finding, not per round), reusing kolu’s codex-exec-lib.sh plumbing nearly verbatim (retry/backoff, synthesized schema-valid error verdict, --sandbox read-only, the MECHANICAL RUNNER agent pattern) while dropping all multi-round machinery — no warm sessions, no resume, no rebuttal files. Effort high, not xhigh: agency#97 shows high is the threshold where codex reviews find real issues; #1222 shows xhigh-every-round is the cost runaway. The packet contains the diff plus each finding’s full claim text and citations, redacting the implementer’s self-assessment and the lens reviewer’s chain of reasoning (the two evidenced correlated-error channels). Codex returns one verdict per finding — confirm, refute (must cite file:line), amend (the remedy as written creates a problem — this is where the cross-lens question now lives), or duplicate (cross-lens semantic dedup, for free).

The evidence gate computes dispositions; nobody negotiates

  • Verified refute (citation checked against the tree by a read-only verifier) → No-op with the evidence recorded.
  • Unverified refute → the fix ships, table row marked contested with codex’s objection verbatim — the human adjudicates at PR review, which is where /do already places the human. Contested findings are apply-and-flag, never No-op: a No-op-and-record disposition would be a schema-blessed Defer.
  • The gate is symmetric: fix-bound findings need code-anchored citations too; confirm-without-evidence renders as such, never as verification.
  • Monotone degradation ladder: codex absent or failing mid-run re-arms the legacy hickey⇄lowy cross-validation prose. A flaky codex can never produce less cross-checking than no codex. The PR comment header carries a mode badge (cross-family / single-family / codex-degraded) so review-depth variance is visible per PR.

One bounded post-apply re-review

The biggest correctness hole in today’s /do: the refactor(*) commits are never reviewed by anyone. The fix is one pass scoped to the refactor-commit diff, verdicts confirm/regress only, regressions fixed once then frozen as flagged table rows — no loop. This closes the hole without importing the #1222 machinery. (kolu’s /be-review has a related structural answer for free — see below.)

Cross-validation: keep, transformed, in two stages

The existing hickey⇄lowy cross-validation is the only cross-check in the whole option space with an empirically validated catch ( #198 ). So: keep it on every tier now, upgraded to run over ledger finding-ids (which mechanically kills its worst defect — cross-validators re-emitting first-pass findings as new rows). Only after a retrospective A/B shows the codex kill-round achieves a meaningful verified kill/amend rate on structural claims does the cross-lens question fold into the kill-round’s amend verdict. The 79–83% kill rate in the literature comes from refutable bug-claims; nobody has measured the transfer to structural-taste claims. Don’t trade the proven check for the bet before the bet pays out. And when the question does move, it must move with the lens methodology loaded — a generic verifier holding neither SKILL.md cannot run the complecting catalog or the documented hickey/lowy conflict-resolution pattern, which is the part that did the work in #198.

Shipping order

  1. Ledger + deterministic table renderer + id’d cross-validation — pure portability, no codex, no Workflow tool; fixes documented failures on every harness.
  2. The retrospective A/B — a throwaway script over ~10 past merged /do PRs, before any codex infrastructure lands.
  3. codex-crosscheck.sh (vendored kolu lib) — if and as the A/B justifies it; plain bash, benefits every tier.
  4. review.workflow.js — last, gated on a smoke test and a non-silent tier-fallback signal. (~200 lines vs. debate.workflow.js’s 512 — no round loop, no warm sessions, no synthesis machinery.)

Cost at tier 1 with findings present: 5–6 agents, ~5–9 min — roughly today’s wall clock, with the two cross-validation forks replaced by one codex call billed to the codex subscription, and reviewer prose no longer burning main context.

What kolu should change

kolu’s review stack is ahead of the field in three ways the evidence endorses: structured JSON consensus detected in code (not vibes), kernel-enforced read-only codex sandboxes, and /lens-debate’s independent-then-debate discipline (the #1109 lesson, independently replicating the published anchoring results). The recommendations below keep all of that and adjust the parts the evidence contradicts.

/be-review: reorder the gauntlet, fix the rationale channel, add a cheap tier

The serial gauntlet (.apm/skills/be-review/SKILL.md) has a property the research values highly and agency’s /do lacks entirely: “each step sees the commits the previous step added… a later reviewer reviews the earlier reviewer’s fixes too.” That is a free re-review pass. But it is currently pointed the wrong way:

  1. Run lens before codex (today: codex → lens → simplify → police). /lens-debate applies structural rewrites — the largest, riskiest commits in the gauntlet — and in today’s order nothing cross-model ever reviews them: only /simplify and /code-police follow, and neither is a correctness reviewer. Flipping to lens → codex → simplify → police makes the cross-family correctness debate cover the lens’s applied refactors, closing the same unreviewed-refactor-commits hole that is the biggest defect in agency’s /do — using machinery /be-review already has. The serial-editor collision argument is order-independent; only the coverage changes.
  2. Thread --rationale into adjudication, not generation. Today the rationale is passed to the lenses “so the lenses don’t flag deliberate decisions” — but author intent and confidence shared with reviewers is the measured 3–4× finding-suppression channel, and pre-loading reviewers is exactly the curation bias /lens-debate’s own #1109 trial proved changes verdicts. The deliberate-decision context belongs at the disposition step: let the lenses flag the deliberate decision cold, then let the rationale justify a wont-fix disposition during cross-examination — visibly, in the debate trail, instead of invisibly, as a finding that was never born.
  3. Add a --quick tier (and consider making it the default). The full gauntlet runs two uncapped/high-backstop debates at xhigh/Opus per PR. The evidence-shaped cheap tier: independent lens generation (parallel, as /lens-debate already does) → merge with stable ids → one cold codex verification of the merged findings (claims-only packet, confirm/refute/amend/duplicate) → evidence gate → serial apply. Fixed agent count, one codex exec, no rounds. Reserve the full debating gauntlet for flagship changes where the marginal value of cross-examination is worth the marginal cost — and the marginal value is highest exactly when the cheap tier’s verdicts come back contested, which the ledger makes visible.
  4. Make codex degradation honest and substituted. Today, three reviewer-error attempts mean “give up on codex… move on” — the run silently loses its only cross-family check. Borrow the monotone-ladder rule: on persistent reviewer-error, substitute a fresh-context same-family correctness pass (fresh-session still beats same-session per Cross-Context Review), and badge the final comments with the degraded mode so review-depth variance is visible on the PR rather than absent.
  5. Share one findings ledger across tracks. Four tracks post four comments and nothing dedups across them — codex and lens can flag the same construct, and police’s elegance pass re-fixes what simplify settled. A run-scoped ledger with stable ids (the .codex-debate/ scratch-dir pattern generalized), each track reading and extending it, would cut double-fixing, give /be-review crash-resume for free, and let the final comments render deterministically instead of being retyped per track.

/codex-debate: cap the review loop, gate concessions on evidence

  • Adopt a round backstop in review mode. /lens-debate already has the right shape — --max-rounds with unresolved as a first-class terminal state that /be-review knows how to adjudicate. Review-mode /codex-debate has neither (“no round cap, no deadlock exit”), and #1222 documents the consequence. The literature adds the sharper point: even converging debates go increasingly wrong after round 2. A backstop of 3 with unresolved reported honestly is not a deadlock exit — quitting without agreement and pretending otherwise is what defeats the purpose; surfacing genuine disagreement to the human is the correct output of a debate that didn’t converge.
  • Evidence-gated concessions. Add a required concessionReason (with a code citation) to both sides’ schemas whenever a disposition flips across rounds. Smit et al. found agreement modulation is the highest-leverage MAD knob, and the conformity literature shows ungated flips are predominantly harmful. A side that cannot cite why it changed its mind hasn’t been convinced; it has capitulated to end the loop.
  • Tier the reasoning effort. xhigh on round 1, high thereafter — the #1222 fix list already proposed this; agency#97 establishes high as the find-real-issues threshold.
  • Answer mode ( #1269 ) shares the loop-to-agreement contract and should get the same two guards (backstop + concession reasons); its parallel-swap confirmation phase is sound and unaffected.

/lens-debate: verify unanimity, tighten the backstop

  • Unanimity is the unguarded path. Findings both lenses agree on round 1 lock as settled and get applied with zero adversarial check — yet hickey and lowy share training family, prompt style, and literally cross-reference each other’s catalogs, so their agreement is the correlated, low-signal kind. Route agreed fix findings through the same single cold codex claims-only check before apply. Agreement should gate debate (skip it — the lenses have nothing to argue), never verification.
  • Debate only on disagreement is already the implicit structure (settled-findings lock); make it explicit and pair it with a tighter backstop — --max-rounds default 3–4 rather than 12. The contested set is monotonically non-increasing, so almost all convergence happens in the first rounds; what’s left at round 4 is a genuine judgment call that unresolved + human adjudication (which /be-review §2 already implements) handles better than rounds 5–12 of bias amplification.
  • Keep independent parallel generation, the settled-findings lock, sequential reveal, and the electricity probe — all four survive contact with the literature, and the first is independently validated by it.

The one-sentence version

Spend the debate budget where disagreement is (contested findings, capped rounds, cited concessions), spend the verification budget where agreement is (cold cross-family kill-rounds on claims-only packets), and never let either budget be spent by a loop whose exit condition is two models getting tired of arguing.

← the Atlas github ↗
